Any S3-compatible client — boto3, AWS CLI, DuckDB, rclone — works without modification.\n6\t\n7\t- **Zero-knowledge encryption** — data is encrypted before it leaves ARMOR; B2 only ever stores ciphertext\n8\t- **Zero egress fees** — downloads route through Cloudflare via the Bandwidth Alliance\n9\t- **Seekable encryption** — AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads without decrypting the whole file\n10\t- **DuckDB-compatible** — query encrypted Parquet files with column pruning and predicate pushdown intact\n11\t- **Multi-key routing** — different master keys for different path prefixes; automatic key selection per object\n12\t\n13\t## Quick Start\n14\t\n15\t### Docker\n16\t\n17\t```bash\n18\tdocker run -d \\\n19\t  -p 9000:9000 \\\n20\t  -p 9001:9001 \\\n21\t  -e ARMOR_B2_REGION=us-east-005 \\\n22\t  -e ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your-key-id \\\n23\t  -e ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your-key-secret \\\n24\t  -e ARMOR_BUCKET=your-bucket \\\n25\t  -e ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN=your-cf-domain.example.com \\\n26\t  -e ARMOR_MEK=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \\\n27\t  -e ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY=my-access-key \\\n28\t  -e ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY=my-secret-key \\\n29\t  ronaldraygun/armor:0.1.43\n30\t```\n31\t\n32\t> **Note:** The ARMOR CI pipeline auto-bumps the VERSION file on every build and publishes the container image as `ronaldraygun/armor:<version>` to Docker Hub. Always pin to a specific version tag in production deployments. Use the latest published tag from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/ronaldraygun/armor/tags).\n33\t\n34\t### Client Configuration\n35\t\n36\tPoint any S3-compatible tool at ARMOR's listen address:\n37\t\n38\t```bash\n39\t# AWS CLI\n40\taws --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3 cp file.txt s3://bucket/key\n41\t\n42\t# boto3 (Python)\n43\timport boto3\n44\ts3 = boto3.client('s3',\n45\t    endpoint_url='http://localhost:9000',\n46\t    aws_access_key_id='my-access-key',\n47\t    aws_secret_access_key='my-secret-key')\n48\ts3.upload_file('local.txt', 'bucket', 'key')\n49\t\n50\t# DuckDB\n51\tINSTALL httpfs;\n52\tLOAD httpfs;\n53\tSET s3_endpoint='localhost:9000';\n54\tSET s3_access_key_id='my-access-key';\n55\tSET s3_secret_access_key='my-secret-key';\n56\tSELECT * FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet');\n57\t```\n58\t\n59\t## Cost Model\n60\t\n61\t| Component | Cost |\n62\t|-----------|------|\n63\t| Storage | ~$6–7/TB/month |\n64\t| Egress (via Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance) | $0 |\n65\t| B2 API calls | $0 |\n66\t| Cloudflare (free plan) | $0 |\n67\t| **Total** | **~$6–7/TB/month** |\n68\t\n69\t## Architecture\n70\t\n71\t### Upload Path (direct to B2 — ingress is free)\n72\t\n73\t```\n74\t┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n75\t│  Client   │────▶│    ARMOR     │────▶│    B2    │\n76\t│           │     │  encrypt +   │     │          │\n77\t│           │     │  upload      │     │          │\n78\t└──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘\n79\t```\n80\t\n81\t### Download Path (through Cloudflare — egress is free)\n82\t\n83\t```\n84\t┌──────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n85\t│  Client   │◀───│ Cloudflare │◀───│  Cloudflare │◀───│    B2    │\n86\t│  ARMOR    │    │   Edge     │    │  PNI Link   │    │          │\n87\t│  decrypt  │    │  (cache)   │    │  (free)     │    │          │\n88\t└──────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘     └──────────┘\n89\t```\n90\t\n91\t### DuckDB Query Path (seekable decryption)\n92\t\n93\tDuckDB issues byte-range GET requests for specific row groups and columns. ARMOR decrypts only the requested 64KB blocks, so column pruning and predicate pushdown remain effective:\n94\t\n95\t```\n96\tDuckDB                          ARMOR                       Cloudflare → B2\n97\t  │                                │                              │\n98\t  ├─ read footer (last 8 bytes) ──▶├─ Range GET (encrypted) ────▶│\n99\t  │◀── decrypted footer ──────────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n100\t  │                                │                              │\n101\t  ├─ read col_a, row group 3 ────▶├─ Range GET (3 blocks) ─────▶│\n102\t  │◀── decrypted column chunk ────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n103\t  │                                │                              │\n104\t  └─ result set                    └                              └\n105\t```\n106\t\n107\t## Encryption Design\n108\t\n109\t```\n110\tMaster Key (MEK)\n111\t │  stored locally, never uploaded\n112\t │\n113\t └─▶ wraps ──▶ Data Encryption Key (DEK)\n114\t                │  random per-file, wrapped copy in B2 metadata\n115\t                │\n116\t                └─▶ encrypts ──▶ File Data\n117\t                                   AES-256-CTR, 64KB blocks\n118\t                                   per-block HMAC-SHA256\n119\t                                   seekable random access\n120\t```\n121\t\n122\tKey rotation re-wraps DEKs without re-uploading file data — a metadata-only operation.\n123\t\n124\t## Security Model\n125\t\n126\t| Threat | Mitigation |\n127\t|--------|-----------|\n128\t| B2 breach | Client-side encryption — B2 only stores opaque blobs |\n129\t| Cloudflare inspection | Client-side encryption — Cloudflare only caches opaque blobs |\n130\t| Man-in-the-middle | TLS everywhere + client-side encryption |\n131\t| Key compromise | Envelope encryption — per-file DEKs limit blast radius; rotation re-wraps without re-uploading |\n132\t| Data corruption | SHA-256 integrity hash + per-block HMACs |\n133\t| Unauthorized access | Private bucket + Cloudflare Worker auth + scoped application keys |\n134\t\n135\t## Configuration\n136\t\n137\tARMOR is configured via environment variables:\n138\t\n139\t| Variable | Required | Default | Description |\n140\t|----------|----------|---------|-------------|\n141\t| `ARMOR_LISTEN` | No | `0.0.0.0:9000` | S3 API listen address |\n142\t| `ARMOR_ADMIN_LISTEN` | No | `127.0.0.1:9001` | Admin API (key rotation, canary, audit) |\n143\t| `ARMOR_B2_REGION` | Yes | — | B2 region (e.g., `us-east-005`) |\n144\t| `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | Yes | — | B2 application key ID |\n145\t| `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | Yes | — | B2 application key |\n146\t| `ARMOR_BUCKET` | Yes | — | B2 bucket name |\n147\t| `ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN` | Yes | — | Cloudflare domain CNAME'd to B2 |\n148\t| `ARMOR_MEK` | Yes | — | Master encryption key (hex, 32 bytes) |\n149\t| `ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY` | No | (random) | Client access key |\n150\t| `ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY` | No | (random) | Client secret key |\n151\t| `ARMOR_BLOCK_SIZE` | No | `65536` | Encryption block size (bytes) |\n152\t| `ARMOR_WRITER_ID` | No | (hostname) | Provenance chain writer ID |\n153\t| `ARMOR_READYZ_CACHE_TTL` | No | `30` | Seconds to cache backend connectivity in `/readyz` |\n154\t| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_USER` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth username |\n155\t| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_PASS` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth password |\n156\t| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` | No | — | Dashboard Bearer token |\n157\t\n158\t### Multi-Key Routing\n159\t\n160\tRoute different path prefixes to different master keys:\n161\t\n162\t```bash\n163\tARMOR_MEK=<hex>                           # default key\n164\tARMOR_MEK_SENSITIVE=<hex>                 # named key\n165\tARMOR_MEK_ARCHIVE=<hex>                   # named key\n166\tARMOR_KEY_ROUTES=\"data/pii/*=sensitive,archive/*=archive,*=default\"\n167\t```\n168\t\n169\t### Multi-Credential Configuration\n170\t\n171\t```bash\n172\tARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACCESS_KEY=reader-key\n173\tARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_SECRET_KEY=reader-secret\n174\tARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACL=\"mybucket:readonly/*\"\n175\t\n176\tARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACCESS_KEY=writer-key\n177\tARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_SECRET_KEY=writer-secret\n178\tARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACL=\"mybucket:*,otherbucket:uploads/*\"\n179\t```\n180\t\n181\t## S3 API Coverage\n182\t\n183\t### Transforming Operations (encryption/decryption applied)\n184\t\n185\t| Operation | Support |\n186\t|-----------|---------|\n187\t| PutObject | Full (streaming for large files) |\n188\t| GetObject | Full (range reads) |\n189\t| HeadObject | Full (plaintext size, conditionals) |\n190\t| CopyObject | Full (DEK re-wrapping, cross-bucket) |\n191\t| CreateMultipartUpload | Full |\n192\t| UploadPart | Full |\n193\t| CompleteMultipartUpload | Full |\n194\t| AbortMultipartUpload | Full |\n195\t| ListParts | Full |\n196\t| ListMultipartUploads | Full |\n197\t\n198\t### Passthrough Operations\n199\t\n200\t| Operation | Support |\n201\t|-----------|---------|\n202\t| ListObjectsV2 | Full (size correction, `.armor/` filter) |\n203\t| DeleteObject | Full |\n204\t| DeleteObjects | Full |\n205\t| ListBuckets | Full |\n206\t| CreateBucket / DeleteBucket / HeadBucket | Full |\n207\t| Lifecycle configuration | Full |\n208\t| Object Lock / Retention / Legal Hold | Full |\n209\t\n210\t**Reserved Namespace: `.armor/`**\n211\t\n212\tThe `.armor/` prefix is reserved for ARMOR internal use. Client operations targeting keys with this prefix return `403 AccessDenied`. This protects:\n213\t\n214\t- `.armor/chain/<writer>/*` — Tamper-evident provenance chain entries\n215\t- `.armor/chain-head/<writer>` — Provenance chain head pointers\n216\t- `.armor/manifest/<writer>/*` — Manifest delta files (IV + wrapped DEK entries)\n217\t- `.armor/hmac/<sha256>` — Multipart upload HMAC sidecars\n218\t- `.armor/rotation-state.json` — In-progress key rotation state\n219\t- `.armor/multipart/*.state` — Crash recovery state for multipart uploads\n220\t- `.armor/canary/*` — Health check canary objects\n221\t\n222\tInternal ARMOR components (provenance recorder, manifest persistence, canary, key rotation, multipart state manager) access these keys directly through the backend layer, bypassing the S3 handler guard.\n223\t\n224\t## Web Dashboard\n225\t\n226\tA web dashboard for bucket browsing, encryption status, and metrics is available on the admin port (default `127.0.0.1:9001`):\n227\t\n228\t```bash\n229\topen http://localhost:9001/dashboard\n230\t```\n231\t\n232\tFeatures:\n233\t- Bucket browsing with prefix-based navigation\n234\t- Encryption status badges per object (key name, ARMOR vs. unencrypted)\n235\t- Metadata cache hit rates\n236\t- Real-time metrics: requests, bytes transferred, uptime, canary status\n237\t\n238\tSee [docs/dashboard.md](docs/dashboard.md) for full documentation.\n239\t\n240\t## Admin API\n241\t\n242\tKey management and monitoring endpoints on the admin listener (`127.0.0.1:9001`):\n243\t\n244\t| Endpoint | Method | Description |\n245\t|----------|--------|-------------|\n246\t| `/healthz` | GET | Liveness check |\n247\t| `/readyz` | GET | Readiness check (verifies B2 connectivity) |\n248\t| `/metrics` | GET | Prometheus metrics |\n249\t| `/admin/key/verify` | GET | Verify MEK can decrypt the canary object |\n250\t| `/admin/key/rotate` | POST | Rotate MEK — re-wraps all DEKs, no file re-upload |\n251\t| `/admin/key/export` | GET | Export current MEK (`?confirm=yes`) |\n252\t| `/admin/audit` | GET | Walk provenance chains, verify integrity |\n253\t| `/admin/presign` | POST | Generate pre-signed share URL |\n254\t| `/armor/canary` | GET | Canary integrity status |\n255\t| `/dashboard` | GET | Web dashboard |\n256\t\n257\t## Repository Structure\n258\t\n259\t```\n260\tARMOR/\n261\t├── cmd/armor/main.go          # Entrypoint\n262\t├── internal/\n263\t│   ├── server/                # S3 API handlers, auth\n264\t│   ├── crypto/                # Encryption, decryption, envelope key management\n265\t│   ├── backend/               # B2 S3 client, Cloudflare download routing\n266\t│   ├── canary/                # Self-healing integrity monitor\n267\t│   ├── config/                # Configuration loading (env vars)\n268\t│   ├── keymanager/            # Multi-key routing\n269\t│   ├── dashboard/             # Web dashboard UI and metrics\n270\t│   ├── presign/               # Pre-signed URL generation\n271\t│   ├── provenance/            # Cryptographic audit chain\n272\t│   ├── logging/               # Structured JSON logging\n273\t│   └── metrics/               # Prometheus metrics\n274\t├── deploy/kubernetes/         # Kubernetes manifests\n275\t├── tests/integration/         # Integration tests (requires real B2 + Cloudflare)\n276\t└── docs/\n277\t    ├── dashboard.md\n278\t    ├── cloudflare-setup.md    # DNS configuration for zero-egress downloads\n279\t    └── research/\n280\t```\n281\t\n282\t## Documentation\n283\t\n284\t- [Disaster Recovery](docs/disaster-recovery.md) — MEK backup/escrow, restore drills, rotation failure recovery\n285\t- [Web Dashboard](docs/dashboard.md) — Bucket browsing, encryption status, cache statistics\n286\t- [Cloudflare Setup](docs/cloudflare-setup.md) — DNS configuration for zero-egress B2 downloads\n287\t- [Integration Tests](tests/integration/README.md) — Testing against real B2 + Cloudflare\n288\t\n289\t## Disaster Recovery / Offline Decryption\n290\t\n291\tARMOR includes a standalone CLI tool `armor-decrypt` for recovering encrypted objects without a running ARMOR server. This enables disaster recovery scenarios where you have:\n292\t\n293\t- The Master Encryption Key (MEK)\n294\t- Access to B2 (or a local copy of an encrypted object)\n295\t\n296\t### Building the Decrypt Tool\n297\t\n298\t```bash\n299\tgo build -o armor-decrypt ./cmd/armor-decrypt\n300\t```\n301\t\n302\t### Usage\n303\t\n304\t#### Decrypt from B2\n305\t\n306\t```bash\n307\t# Decrypt directly from B2 (requires B2 credentials)\n308\tarmor-decrypt \\\n309\t  -mek 0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef \\\n310\t  -input b2://my-bucket/path/to/file.encrypted \\\n311\t  -output recovered-file.txt\n312\t\n313\t# Using MEK from environment\n314\texport ARMOR_MEK=0123456789abcdef...\n315\tarmor-decrypt -input b2://my-bucket/file -output recovered.txt\n316\t\n317\t# With verbose output\n318\tarmor-decrypt -mek HEX -input b2://bucket/file -v - output recovered.txt\n319\t```\n320\t\n321\t#### Decrypt from Local File\n322\t\n323\tFor local files, you need the wrapped DEK (from `x-amz-meta-armor-wrapped-dek` metadata):\n324\t\n325\t```bash\n326\tarmor-decrypt \\\n327\t  -mek 0123456789abcdef... \\\n328\t  -input /path/to/encrypted.bin \\\n329\t  -wrapped-dek WWF...base64... \\\n330\t  -output plaintext.bin\n331\t```\n332\t\n333\t### Key Requirements\n334\t\n335\t- **MEK (Master Encryption Key)**: 32-byte hex string\n336\t- **For B2**: `ARMOR_B2_REGION`, `ARMOR_B2_ENDPOINT`, `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`\n337\t- **For local files**: Wrapped DEK (base64, from object metadata)\n338\t\n339\t### Multi-Key Support\n340\t\n341\tIf your ARMOR deployment uses named keys (via `ARMOR_KEY_ROUTES`), specify the key ID:\n342\t\n343\t```bash\n344\tarmor-decrypt \\\n345\t  -mek <hex-for-specific-key> \\\n346\t  -input b2://bucket/file \\\n347\t  -key-id sensitive \\\n348\t  -output recovered.txt\n349\t```\n350\t\n351\tThe key ID comes from the `x-amz-meta-armor-key-id` metadata header.\n352\t\n353\t### Verification\n354\t\n355\tThe decrypt tool automatically:\n356\t\n357\t- Verifies per-block HMAC-SHA256 integrity\n358\t- Validates the plaintext SHA-256 checksum\n359\t- Detects corrupted blocks or wrong MEK\n360\t\n361\tExit codes:\n362\t- `0`: Success\n363\t- `1`: Decryption failed (wrong MEK, corrupted data, HMAC mismatch)\n364\t\n365\t### Example Workflow\n366\t\n367\t```bash\n368\t# 1. List objects to find the target\n369\taws s3 ls --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3://bucket/\n370\t\n371\t# 2. Get metadata to see key requirements\n372\taws s3api head-object --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 \\\n373\t  --bucket bucket --key file\n374\t\n375\t# 3. Decrypt with the correct MEK\n376\tarmor-decrypt -mek $ARMOR_MEK -input b2://bucket/file -output recovered\n377\t\n378\t# 4. Verify the recovered file\n379\tsha256sum recovered  # Should match x-amz-meta-armor-plaintext-sha256\n380\t```\n381\t\n382\t## License\n383\t\n384\tMIT\n385\t"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"49f70665-f886-42b4-9617-a7d0e564b452","uuid":"917dafd4-c515-4126-9132-aed67c581d9b","timestamp":"2026-07-15T14:01:18.631Z","tool_use_result":{"type":"text","file":{"filePath":"/home/coding/ARMOR/README.md","content":"# ARMOR\n\n**Authenticated Range-readable Managed Object Repository**\n\nARMOR is an S3-compatible proxy server that transparently encrypts data before storing it in [Backblaze B2](https://www.backblaze.com/cloud-storage) and serves downloads through Cloudflare for zero-egress cost. Any S3-compatible client — boto3, AWS CLI, DuckDB, rclone — works without modification.\n\n- **Zero-knowledge encryption** — data is encrypted before it leaves ARMOR; B2 only ever stores ciphertext\n- **Zero egress fees** — downloads route through Cloudflare via the Bandwidth Alliance\n- **Seekable encryption** — AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads without decrypting the whole file\n- **DuckDB-compatible** — query encrypted Parquet files with column pruning and predicate pushdown intact\n- **Multi-key routing** — different master keys for different path prefixes; automatic key selection per object\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### Docker\n\n```bash\ndocker run -d \\\n  -p 9000:9000 \\\n  -p 9001:9001 \\\n  -e ARMOR_B2_REGION=us-east-005 \\\n  -e ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID=your-key-id \\\n  -e ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=your-key-secret \\\n  -e ARMOR_BUCKET=your-bucket \\\n  -e ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN=your-cf-domain.example.com \\\n  -e ARMOR_MEK=$(openssl rand -hex 32) \\\n  -e ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY=my-access-key \\\n  -e ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY=my-secret-key \\\n  ronaldraygun/armor:0.1.43\n```\n\n> **Note:** The ARMOR CI pipeline auto-bumps the VERSION file on every build and publishes the container image as `ronaldraygun/armor:<version>` to Docker Hub. Always pin to a specific version tag in production deployments. Use the latest published tag from [Docker Hub](https://hub.docker.com/r/ronaldraygun/armor/tags).\n\n### Client Configuration\n\nPoint any S3-compatible tool at ARMOR's listen address:\n\n```bash\n# AWS CLI\naws --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3 cp file.txt s3://bucket/key\n\n# boto3 (Python)\nimport boto3\ns3 = boto3.client('s3',\n    endpoint_url='http://localhost:9000',\n    aws_access_key_id='my-access-key',\n    aws_secret_access_key='my-secret-key')\ns3.upload_file('local.txt', 'bucket', 'key')\n\n# DuckDB\nINSTALL httpfs;\nLOAD httpfs;\nSET s3_endpoint='localhost:9000';\nSET s3_access_key_id='my-access-key';\nSET s3_secret_access_key='my-secret-key';\nSELECT * FROM read_parquet('s3://bucket/data.parquet');\n```\n\n## Cost Model\n\n| Component | Cost |\n|-----------|------|\n| Storage | ~$6–7/TB/month |\n| Egress (via Cloudflare Bandwidth Alliance) | $0 |\n| B2 API calls | $0 |\n| Cloudflare (free plan) | $0 |\n| **Total** | **~$6–7/TB/month** |\n\n## Architecture\n\n### Upload Path (direct to B2 — ingress is free)\n\n```\n┌──────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n│  Client   │────▶│    ARMOR     │────▶│    B2    │\n│           │     │  encrypt +   │     │          │\n│           │     │  upload      │     │          │\n└──────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────┘\n```\n\n### Download Path (through Cloudflare — egress is free)\n\n```\n┌──────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌──────────┐\n│  Client   │◀───│ Cloudflare │◀───│  Cloudflare │◀───│    B2    │\n│  ARMOR    │    │   Edge     │    │  PNI Link   │    │          │\n│  decrypt  │    │  (cache)   │    │  (free)     │    │          │\n└──────────┘     └────────────┘     └────────────┘     └──────────┘\n```\n\n### DuckDB Query Path (seekable decryption)\n\nDuckDB issues byte-range GET requests for specific row groups and columns. ARMOR decrypts only the requested 64KB blocks, so column pruning and predicate pushdown remain effective:\n\n```\nDuckDB                          ARMOR                       Cloudflare → B2\n  │                                │                              │\n  ├─ read footer (last 8 bytes) ──▶├─ Range GET (encrypted) ────▶│\n  │◀── decrypted footer ──────────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n  │                                │                              │\n  ├─ read col_a, row group 3 ────▶├─ Range GET (3 blocks) ─────▶│\n  │◀── decrypted column chunk ────┤◀── ciphertext ──────────────┤\n  │                                │                              │\n  └─ result set                    └                              └\n```\n\n## Encryption Design\n\n```\nMaster Key (MEK)\n │  stored locally, never uploaded\n │\n └─▶ wraps ──▶ Data Encryption Key (DEK)\n                │  random per-file, wrapped copy in B2 metadata\n                │\n                └─▶ encrypts ──▶ File Data\n                                   AES-256-CTR, 64KB blocks\n                                   per-block HMAC-SHA256\n                                   seekable random access\n```\n\nKey rotation re-wraps DEKs without re-uploading file data — a metadata-only operation.\n\n## Security Model\n\n| Threat | Mitigation |\n|--------|-----------|\n| B2 breach | Client-side encryption — B2 only stores opaque blobs |\n| Cloudflare inspection | Client-side encryption — Cloudflare only caches opaque blobs |\n| Man-in-the-middle | TLS everywhere + client-side encryption |\n| Key compromise | Envelope encryption — per-file DEKs limit blast radius; rotation re-wraps without re-uploading |\n| Data corruption | SHA-256 integrity hash + per-block HMACs |\n| Unauthorized access | Private bucket + Cloudflare Worker auth + scoped application keys |\n\n## Configuration\n\nARMOR is configured via environment variables:\n\n| Variable | Required | Default | Description |\n|----------|----------|---------|-------------|\n| `ARMOR_LISTEN` | No | `0.0.0.0:9000` | S3 API listen address |\n| `ARMOR_ADMIN_LISTEN` | No | `127.0.0.1:9001` | Admin API (key rotation, canary, audit) |\n| `ARMOR_B2_REGION` | Yes | — | B2 region (e.g., `us-east-005`) |\n| `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID` | Yes | — | B2 application key ID |\n| `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` | Yes | — | B2 application key |\n| `ARMOR_BUCKET` | Yes | — | B2 bucket name |\n| `ARMOR_CF_DOMAIN` | Yes | — | Cloudflare domain CNAME'd to B2 |\n| `ARMOR_MEK` | Yes | — | Master encryption key (hex, 32 bytes) |\n| `ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY` | No | (random) | Client access key |\n| `ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY` | No | (random) | Client secret key |\n| `ARMOR_BLOCK_SIZE` | No | `65536` | Encryption block size (bytes) |\n| `ARMOR_WRITER_ID` | No | (hostname) | Provenance chain writer ID |\n| `ARMOR_READYZ_CACHE_TTL` | No | `30` | Seconds to cache backend connectivity in `/readyz` |\n| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_USER` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth username |\n| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_PASS` | No | — | Dashboard HTTP Basic Auth password |\n| `ARMOR_DASHBOARD_TOKEN` | No | — | Dashboard Bearer token |\n\n### Multi-Key Routing\n\nRoute different path prefixes to different master keys:\n\n```bash\nARMOR_MEK=<hex>                           # default key\nARMOR_MEK_SENSITIVE=<hex>                 # named key\nARMOR_MEK_ARCHIVE=<hex>                   # named key\nARMOR_KEY_ROUTES=\"data/pii/*=sensitive,archive/*=archive,*=default\"\n```\n\n### Multi-Credential Configuration\n\n```bash\nARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACCESS_KEY=reader-key\nARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_SECRET_KEY=reader-secret\nARMOR_AUTH_READONLY_ACL=\"mybucket:readonly/*\"\n\nARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACCESS_KEY=writer-key\nARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_SECRET_KEY=writer-secret\nARMOR_AUTH_WRITER_ACL=\"mybucket:*,otherbucket:uploads/*\"\n```\n\n## S3 API Coverage\n\n### Transforming Operations (encryption/decryption applied)\n\n| Operation | Support |\n|-----------|---------|\n| PutObject | Full (streaming for large files) |\n| GetObject | Full (range reads) |\n| HeadObject | Full (plaintext size, conditionals) |\n| CopyObject | Full (DEK re-wrapping, cross-bucket) |\n| CreateMultipartUpload | Full |\n| UploadPart | Full |\n| CompleteMultipartUpload | Full |\n| AbortMultipartUpload | Full |\n| ListParts | Full |\n| ListMultipartUploads | Full |\n\n### Passthrough Operations\n\n| Operation | Support |\n|-----------|---------|\n| ListObjectsV2 | Full (size correction, `.armor/` filter) |\n| DeleteObject | Full |\n| DeleteObjects | Full |\n| ListBuckets | Full |\n| CreateBucket / DeleteBucket / HeadBucket | Full |\n| Lifecycle configuration | Full |\n| Object Lock / Retention / Legal Hold | Full |\n\n**Reserved Namespace: `.armor/`**\n\nThe `.armor/` prefix is reserved for ARMOR internal use. Client operations targeting keys with this prefix return `403 AccessDenied`. This protects:\n\n- `.armor/chain/<writer>/*` — Tamper-evident provenance chain entries\n- `.armor/chain-head/<writer>` — Provenance chain head pointers\n- `.armor/manifest/<writer>/*` — Manifest delta files (IV + wrapped DEK entries)\n- `.armor/hmac/<sha256>` — Multipart upload HMAC sidecars\n- `.armor/rotation-state.json` — In-progress key rotation state\n- `.armor/multipart/*.state` — Crash recovery state for multipart uploads\n- `.armor/canary/*` — Health check canary objects\n\nInternal ARMOR components (provenance recorder, manifest persistence, canary, key rotation, multipart state manager) access these keys directly through the backend layer, bypassing the S3 handler guard.\n\n## Web Dashboard\n\nA web dashboard for bucket browsing, encryption status, and metrics is available on the admin port (default `127.0.0.1:9001`):\n\n```bash\nopen http://localhost:9001/dashboard\n```\n\nFeatures:\n- Bucket browsing with prefix-based navigation\n- Encryption status badges per object (key name, ARMOR vs. unencrypted)\n- Metadata cache hit rates\n- Real-time metrics: requests, bytes transferred, uptime, canary status\n\nSee [docs/dashboard.md](docs/dashboard.md) for full documentation.\n\n## Admin API\n\nKey management and monitoring endpoints on the admin listener (`127.0.0.1:9001`):\n\n| Endpoint | Method | Description |\n|----------|--------|-------------|\n| `/healthz` | GET | Liveness check |\n| `/readyz` | GET | Readiness check (verifies B2 connectivity) |\n| `/metrics` | GET | Prometheus metrics |\n| `/admin/key/verify` | GET | Verify MEK can decrypt the canary object |\n| `/admin/key/rotate` | POST | Rotate MEK — re-wraps all DEKs, no file re-upload |\n| `/admin/key/export` | GET | Export current MEK (`?confirm=yes`) |\n| `/admin/audit` | GET | Walk provenance chains, verify integrity |\n| `/admin/presign` | POST | Generate pre-signed share URL |\n| `/armor/canary` | GET | Canary integrity status |\n| `/dashboard` | GET | Web dashboard |\n\n## Repository Structure\n\n```\nARMOR/\n├── cmd/armor/main.go          # Entrypoint\n├── internal/\n│   ├── server/                # S3 API handlers, auth\n│   ├── crypto/                # Encryption, decryption, envelope key management\n│   ├── backend/               # B2 S3 client, Cloudflare download routing\n│   ├── canary/                # Self-healing integrity monitor\n│   ├── config/                # Configuration loading (env vars)\n│   ├── keymanager/            # Multi-key routing\n│   ├── dashboard/             # Web dashboard UI and metrics\n│   ├── presign/               # Pre-signed URL generation\n│   ├── provenance/            # Cryptographic audit chain\n│   ├── logging/               # Structured JSON logging\n│   └── metrics/               # Prometheus metrics\n├── deploy/kubernetes/         # Kubernetes manifests\n├── tests/integration/         # Integration tests (requires real B2 + Cloudflare)\n└── docs/\n    ├── dashboard.md\n    ├── cloudflare-setup.md    # DNS configuration for zero-egress downloads\n    └── research/\n```\n\n## Documentation\n\n- [Disaster Recovery](docs/disaster-recovery.md) — MEK backup/escrow, restore drills, rotation failure recovery\n- [Web Dashboard](docs/dashboard.md) — Bucket browsing, encryption status, cache statistics\n- [Cloudflare Setup](docs/cloudflare-setup.md) — DNS configuration for zero-egress B2 downloads\n- [Integration Tests](tests/integration/README.md) — Testing against real B2 + Cloudflare\n\n## Disaster Recovery / Offline Decryption\n\nARMOR includes a standalone CLI tool `armor-decrypt` for recovering encrypted objects without a running ARMOR server. This enables disaster recovery scenarios where you have:\n\n- The Master Encryption Key (MEK)\n- Access to B2 (or a local copy of an encrypted object)\n\n### Building the Decrypt Tool\n\n```bash\ngo build -o armor-decrypt ./cmd/armor-decrypt\n```\n\n### Usage\n\n#### Decrypt from B2\n\n```bash\n# Decrypt directly from B2 (requires B2 credentials)\narmor-decrypt \\\n  -mek 0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef0123456789abcdef \\\n  -input b2://my-bucket/path/to/file.encrypted \\\n  -output recovered-file.txt\n\n# Using MEK from environment\nexport ARMOR_MEK=0123456789abcdef...\narmor-decrypt -input b2://my-bucket/file -output recovered.txt\n\n# With verbose output\narmor-decrypt -mek HEX -input b2://bucket/file -v - output recovered.txt\n```\n\n#### Decrypt from Local File\n\nFor local files, you need the wrapped DEK (from `x-amz-meta-armor-wrapped-dek` metadata):\n\n```bash\narmor-decrypt \\\n  -mek 0123456789abcdef... \\\n  -input /path/to/encrypted.bin \\\n  -wrapped-dek WWF...base64... \\\n  -output plaintext.bin\n```\n\n### Key Requirements\n\n- **MEK (Master Encryption Key)**: 32-byte hex string\n- **For B2**: `ARMOR_B2_REGION`, `ARMOR_B2_ENDPOINT`, `ARMOR_B2_ACCESS_KEY_ID`, `ARMOR_B2_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`\n- **For local files**: Wrapped DEK (base64, from object metadata)\n\n### Multi-Key Support\n\nIf your ARMOR deployment uses named keys (via `ARMOR_KEY_ROUTES`), specify the key ID:\n\n```bash\narmor-decrypt \\\n  -mek <hex-for-specific-key> \\\n  -input b2://bucket/file \\\n  -key-id sensitive \\\n  -output recovered.txt\n```\n\nThe key ID comes from the `x-amz-meta-armor-key-id` metadata header.\n\n### Verification\n\nThe decrypt tool automatically:\n\n- Verifies per-block HMAC-SHA256 integrity\n- Validates the plaintext SHA-256 checksum\n- Detects corrupted blocks or wrong MEK\n\nExit codes:\n- `0`: Success\n- `1`: Decryption failed (wrong MEK, corrupted data, HMAC mismatch)\n\n### Example Workflow\n\n```bash\n# 1. List objects to find the target\naws s3 ls --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 s3://bucket/\n\n# 2. Get metadata to see key requirements\naws s3api head-object --endpoint-url http://localhost:9000 \\\n  --bucket bucket --key file\n\n# 3. Decrypt with the correct MEK\narmor-decrypt -mek $ARMOR_MEK -input b2://bucket/file -output recovered\n\n# 4. Verify the recovered file\nsha256sum recovered  # Should match x-amz-meta-armor-plaintext-sha256\n```\n\n## License\n\nMIT\n","numLines":385,"startLine":1,"totalLines":385}}}

For this restore environment:\n16\t\n17\t| Component | Endpoint | Description |\n18\t|-----------|----------|-------------|\n19\t| **S3 API** | `http://100.80.255.8:9000` | Main ARMOR S3-compatible endpoint for backup storage |\n20\t| **S3 Bucket** | `devimprint` | Bucket containing litestream backups |\n21\t| **Backup Path** | `state/litestream/queue.db` | Litestream replica path within bucket |\n22\t| **Admin API** | `127.0.0.1:9001` | Admin endpoint (not used for restore) |\n23\t| **Dashboard** | `http://localhost:9001/dashboard` | Web UI for browsing and metrics |\n24\t\n25\t### ARMOR Features\n26\t\n27\t- **Zero-knowledge encryption**: Data encrypted before leaving ARMOR\n28\t- **Zero egress fees**: Downloads route through Cloudflare\n29\t- **Seekable encryption**: AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads\n30\t- **S3-compatible**: Works with any S3 client (boto3, AWS CLI, litestream, DuckDB)\n31\t\n32\t## Quick Start\n33\t\n34\t### 1. Set Credentials\n35\t\n36\tThe restore requires ARMOR S3 credentials from the `armor-writer` secret in the `devimprint` namespace.\n37\t\n38\t**IMPORTANT:** The kubectl proxy (`http://kubectl-proxy-ord-devimprint:8001`) has **read-only access** and cannot access secrets.\n39\t\n40\tYou have two options to get the credentials:\n41\t\n42\t#### Option A: Use a kubeconfig with write access (if available)\n43\t\n44\t```bash\n45\t# If you have a kubeconfig with write access to ord-devimprint:\n46\tkubectl get secret armor-writer -n devimprint -o jsonpath='{.data.auth-access-key}' | base64 -d\n47\tkubectl get secret armor-writer -n devimprint -o jsonpath='{.data.auth-secret-key}' | base64 -d\n48\t```\n49\t\n50\t#### Option B: Manual credential input\n51\t\n52\tIf you have access to the credentials through another method, set them as environment variables:\n53\t\n54\t```bash\n55\texport LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<your-access-key>\n56\texport LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<your-secret-key>\n57\t```\n58\t\n59\t### 2. Run the Restore\n60\t\n61\tThe `restore.sh` script is a generic litestream restore utility:\n62\t\n63\t```bash\n64\tcd /home/coding/scratch/fresh-restore\n65\t./restore.sh s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db ./restored/queue.db\n66\t```\n67\t\n68\tThis will:\n69\t- Download the latest backup from ARMOR's S3 endpoint\n70\t- Restore it to `./restored/queue.db`\n71\t- Verify SQLite integrity\n72\t- Display database schema and row counts\n73\t\n74\t### 3. Inspect the Restored Database\n75\t\n76\t```bash\n77\t# Using sqlite3\n78\t/nix/store/pfaam25wgs7ls2bfmq8mdqdmnhbk7j2f-sqlite-3.51.2-bin/bin/sqlite3 ./restored/queue.db\n79\t\n80\t# In sqlite shell:\n81\t.schema                    # Show schema\n82\tSELECT * FROM <table> LIMIT 10;  # Query data\n83\t.quit                      # Exit\n84\t```\n85\t\n86\t## Script Details\n87\t\n88\t### restore.sh\n89\t\n90\tGeneric litestream restore script that takes two positional arguments:\n91\t- **backup-path**: S3 path or file path to the litestream backup\n92\t- **output-db-path**: Where to write the restored SQLite database\n93\t\n94\t**Usage:**\n95\t```bash\n96\t./restore.sh <backup-path> <output-db-path>\n97\t```\n98\t\n99\t**Examples:**\n100\t```bash\n101\t# Restore from S3 (ARMOR endpoint)\n102\t./restore.sh s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db ./restored/queue.db\n103\t\n104\t# Restore from local file\n105\t./restore.sh /backup/path/litestream.db ./restored/database.db\n106\t```\n107\t\n108\t**Requirements:**\n109\t- `litestream` - Install: `go install github.com/benbjohnson/litestream/cmd/litestream@latest`\n110\t- `LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID` and `LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` environment variables (for S3 restores)\n111\t\n112\t**Environment Variables:**\n113\t```bash\n114\t# Required for S3 restores\n115\texport LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<your-access-key>\n116\texport LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<your-secret-key>\n117\t\n118\t# Optional: Set custom S3 endpoint (default: AWS S3)\n119\texport LITESTREAM_ENDPOINT_URL=http://100.80.255.8:9000\n120\t```\n121\t\n122\t## Configuration\n123\t\n124\t### ARMOR S3 Configuration for Restore\n125\t\n126\t```yaml\n127\t# ARMOR S3 Endpoint (litestream connects here)\n128\tS3 Endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000\n129\tS3 Bucket: devimprint\n130\tS3 Path: state/litestream/queue.db\n131\tDatabase: queue.db\n132\t\n133\t# Credentials (from armor-writer secret)\n134\tAccess Key ID: ${LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID}\n135\tSecret Key: ${LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}\n136\t```\n137\t\n138\t### Setting Custom S3 Endpoint\n139\t\n140\tBy default, litestream uses AWS S3 endpoints. To restore from ARMOR:\n141\t\n142\t```bash\n143\t# Method 1: Environment variable (recommended)\n144\texport LITESTREAM_ENDPOINT_URL=http://100.80.255.8:9000\n145\t./restore.sh s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db ./restored/queue.db\n146\t\n147\t# Method 2: Litestream config file (advanced)\n148\tcat > litestream.yml <<EOF\n149\tdbs:\n150\t  - path: ./restored/queue.db\n151\t    replicas:\n152\t      - type: s3\n153\t        endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000\n154\t        bucket: devimprint\n155\t        path: state/litestream/queue.db\n156\tEOF\n157\tlitestream restore -c litestream.yml s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db -o ./restored/queue.db\n158\t```\n159\t\n160\t### Production Litestream Configuration\n161\t\n162\tThe production queue-api deployment uses this configuration:\n163\t\n164\t```yaml\n165\t# queue-api litestream sidecar configuration\n166\tdbs:\n167\t  - path: /data/queue.db\n168\t    replicas:\n169\t      - type: s3\n170\t        endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000  # ARMOR S3 endpoint\n171\t        bucket: devimprint\n172\t        path: state/litestream/queue.db\n173\t        access-key-id: ${ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY}\n174\t        secret-access-key: ${ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY}\n175\t```\n176\t\n177\t## Troubleshooting\n178\t\n179\t### \"S3 credentials not set\"\n180\t\n181\tThe script requires S3 credentials to be set as environment variables:\n182\t```bash\n183\texport LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<your-key>\n184\texport LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<your-secret>\n185\t```\n186\t\n187\tThese credentials are stored in the `armor-writer` secret in the `devimprint` namespace, but the kubectl proxy has read-only access and cannot access secrets.\n188\t\n189\t### \"litestream not found\"\n190\t\n191\tInstall litestream:\n192\t```bash\n193\tgo install github.com/benbjohnson/litestream/cmd/litestream@latest\n194\t```\n195\t\n196\tVerify installation:\n197\t```bash\n198\tlitestream version\n199\t```\n200\t\n201\t### \"sqlite3 not found\"\n202\t\n203\tThe script uses sqlite3 from the nix store. If you need a different version:\n204\t```bash\n205\t# Find available sqlite versions\n206\tfind /nix/store -name \"sqlite\" -type f -executable\n207\t\n208\t# Or use the sqlite3 binary from nix directly\n209\t/nix/store/pfaam25wgs7ls2bfmq8mdqdmnhbk7j2f-sqlite-3.51.2-bin/bin/sqlite3 --version\n210\t```\n211\t\n212\t### \"Restore failed\" - Network Issues\n213\t\n214\tTest ARMOR endpoint connectivity:\n215\t\n216\t```bash\n217\t# Test basic connectivity\n218\tcurl -I http://100.80.255.8:9000\n219\t\n220\t# Expected response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK or similar\n221\t# If connection refused/timeout: ARMOR may be down or unreachable\n222\t```\n223\t\n224\tCheck if ARMOR is running:\n225\t\n226\t```bash\n227\t# If you have kubectl access to ord-devimprint\n228\tkubectl get pods -n devimprint -l app=armor\n229\t\n230\t# Check ARMOR logs\n231\tkubectl logs -n devimprint -l app=armor --tail=50\n232\t```\n233\t\n234\t### \"Restore failed\" - Authentication Issues\n235\t\n236\tVerify credentials are correct:\n237\t\n238\t```bash\n239\t# Test credentials with AWS CLI (if available)\n240\taws s3 ls \\\n241\t  --endpoint-url http://100.80.255.8:9000 \\\n242\t  --access-key-id $LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID \\\n243\t  --secret-key $LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\n244\t\n245\t# Should list buckets including \"devimprint\"\n246\t```\n247\t\n248\t### \"Restore failed\" - Path Issues\n249\t\n250\tVerify the backup path exists:\n251\t\n252\t```bash\n253\t# List objects in the bucket\n254\taws s3 ls s3://devimprint/state/litestream/ \\\n255\t  --endpoint-url http://100.80.255.8:9000 \\\n256\t  --access-key-id $LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID \\\n257\t  --secret-key $LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\n258\t\n259\t# Should see queue.db and related litestream metadata files\n260\t```\n261\t\n262\t### Database Corruption\n263\t\n264\tIf the restored database fails integrity checks:\n265\t\n266\t```bash\n267\t# Run full integrity check\n268\tsqlite3 ./restored/queue.db \"PRAGMA integrity_check;\"\n269\t\n270\t# If errors reported, try restoring an older backup\n271\t# List available generations in litestream metadata\n272\t```\n273\t\n274\t### ARMOR Endpoint Returns 403/401\n275\t\n276\t- **403 Forbidden**: Check bucket permissions, verify `armor-writer` credentials include read access\n277\t- **401 Unauthorized**: Invalid credentials, verify `LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID` and `LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`\n278\t- **Connection timeout**: ARMOR endpoint unreachable, check network connectivity and service status\n279\t\n280\t## Architecture\n281\t\n282\t### Production Setup\n283\t\n284\t```\n285\tqueue-api Pod (devimprint namespace)\n286\t├── queue-api container\n287\t│   └── /data/queue.db (SQLite database)\n288\t└── litestream sidecar\n289\t    ├── Reads: /data/queue.db\n290\t    ├── Replicates to: ARMOR S3 endpoint\n291\t    └── ARMOR endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000\n292\t         └── Bucket: devimprint\n293\t            └── Path: state/litestream/queue.db\n294\t               └── ARMOR encrypts → Backblaze B2 (ciphertext)\n295\t\n296\tPVC: queue-api-data-sata-2\n297\tSecret: armor-writer (contains ARMOR auth credentials)\n298\t```\n299\t\n300\t### ARMOR in the Data Flow\n301\t\n302\t```\n303\t┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐\n304\t│ queue-api    │────▶│ litestream   │────▶│ ARMOR        │────▶│ Backblaze B2│\n305\t│              │     │ (sidecar)    │     │ (encrypts)   │     │ (storage) For this restore environment:\n\n| Component | Endpoint | Description |\n|-----------|----------|-------------|\n| **S3 API** | `http://100.80.255.8:9000` | Main ARMOR S3-compatible endpoint for backup storage |\n| **S3 Bucket** | `devimprint` | Bucket containing litestream backups |\n| **Backup Path** | `state/litestream/queue.db` | Litestream replica path within bucket |\n| **Admin API** | `127.0.0.1:9001` | Admin endpoint (not used for restore) |\n| **Dashboard** | `http://localhost:9001/dashboard` | Web UI for browsing and metrics |\n\n### ARMOR Features\n\n- **Zero-knowledge encryption**: Data encrypted before leaving ARMOR\n- **Zero egress fees**: Downloads route through Cloudflare\n- **Seekable encryption**: AES-256-CTR with 64KB blocks enables byte-range reads\n- **S3-compatible**: Works with any S3 client (boto3, AWS CLI, litestream, DuckDB)\n\n## Quick Start\n\n### 1. Set Credentials\n\nThe restore requires ARMOR S3 credentials from the `armor-writer` secret in the `devimprint` namespace.\n\n**IMPORTANT:** The kubectl proxy (`http://kubectl-proxy-ord-devimprint:8001`) has **read-only access** and cannot access secrets.\n\nYou have two options to get the credentials:\n\n#### Option A: Use a kubeconfig with write access (if available)\n\n```bash\n# If you have a kubeconfig with write access to ord-devimprint:\nkubectl get secret armor-writer -n devimprint -o jsonpath='{.data.auth-access-key}' | base64 -d\nkubectl get secret armor-writer -n devimprint -o jsonpath='{.data.auth-secret-key}' | base64 -d\n```\n\n#### Option B: Manual credential input\n\nIf you have access to the credentials through another method, set them as environment variables:\n\n```bash\nexport LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<your-access-key>\nexport LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<your-secret-key>\n```\n\n### 2. Run the Restore\n\nThe `restore.sh` script is a generic litestream restore utility:\n\n```bash\ncd /home/coding/scratch/fresh-restore\n./restore.sh s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db ./restored/queue.db\n```\n\nThis will:\n- Download the latest backup from ARMOR's S3 endpoint\n- Restore it to `./restored/queue.db`\n- Verify SQLite integrity\n- Display database schema and row counts\n\n### 3. Inspect the Restored Database\n\n```bash\n# Using sqlite3\n/nix/store/pfaam25wgs7ls2bfmq8mdqdmnhbk7j2f-sqlite-3.51.2-bin/bin/sqlite3 ./restored/queue.db\n\n# In sqlite shell:\n.schema                    # Show schema\nSELECT * FROM <table> LIMIT 10;  # Query data\n.quit                      # Exit\n```\n\n## Script Details\n\n### restore.sh\n\nGeneric litestream restore script that takes two positional arguments:\n- **backup-path**: S3 path or file path to the litestream backup\n- **output-db-path**: Where to write the restored SQLite database\n\n**Usage:**\n```bash\n./restore.sh <backup-path> <output-db-path>\n```\n\n**Examples:**\n```bash\n# Restore from S3 (ARMOR endpoint)\n./restore.sh s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db ./restored/queue.db\n\n# Restore from local file\n./restore.sh /backup/path/litestream.db ./restored/database.db\n```\n\n**Requirements:**\n- `litestream` - Install: `go install github.com/benbjohnson/litestream/cmd/litestream@latest`\n- `LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID` and `LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY` environment variables (for S3 restores)\n\n**Environment Variables:**\n```bash\n# Required for S3 restores\nexport LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<your-access-key>\nexport LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<your-secret-key>\n\n# Optional: Set custom S3 endpoint (default: AWS S3)\nexport LITESTREAM_ENDPOINT_URL=http://100.80.255.8:9000\n```\n\n## Configuration\n\n### ARMOR S3 Configuration for Restore\n\n```yaml\n# ARMOR S3 Endpoint (litestream connects here)\nS3 Endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000\nS3 Bucket: devimprint\nS3 Path: state/litestream/queue.db\nDatabase: queue.db\n\n# Credentials (from armor-writer secret)\nAccess Key ID: ${LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID}\nSecret Key: ${LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY}\n```\n\n### Setting Custom S3 Endpoint\n\nBy default, litestream uses AWS S3 endpoints. To restore from ARMOR:\n\n```bash\n# Method 1: Environment variable (recommended)\nexport LITESTREAM_ENDPOINT_URL=http://100.80.255.8:9000\n./restore.sh s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db ./restored/queue.db\n\n# Method 2: Litestream config file (advanced)\ncat > litestream.yml <<EOF\ndbs:\n  - path: ./restored/queue.db\n    replicas:\n      - type: s3\n        endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000\n        bucket: devimprint\n        path: state/litestream/queue.db\nEOF\nlitestream restore -c litestream.yml s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db -o ./restored/queue.db\n```\n\n### Production Litestream Configuration\n\nThe production queue-api deployment uses this configuration:\n\n```yaml\n# queue-api litestream sidecar configuration\ndbs:\n  - path: /data/queue.db\n    replicas:\n      - type: s3\n        endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000  # ARMOR S3 endpoint\n        bucket: devimprint\n        path: state/litestream/queue.db\n        access-key-id: ${ARMOR_AUTH_ACCESS_KEY}\n        secret-access-key: ${ARMOR_AUTH_SECRET_KEY}\n```\n\n## Troubleshooting\n\n### \"S3 credentials not set\"\n\nThe script requires S3 credentials to be set as environment variables:\n```bash\nexport LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID=<your-key>\nexport LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY=<your-secret>\n```\n\nThese credentials are stored in the `armor-writer` secret in the `devimprint` namespace, but the kubectl proxy has read-only access and cannot access secrets.\n\n### \"litestream not found\"\n\nInstall litestream:\n```bash\ngo install github.com/benbjohnson/litestream/cmd/litestream@latest\n```\n\nVerify installation:\n```bash\nlitestream version\n```\n\n### \"sqlite3 not found\"\n\nThe script uses sqlite3 from the nix store. If you need a different version:\n```bash\n# Find available sqlite versions\nfind /nix/store -name \"sqlite\" -type f -executable\n\n# Or use the sqlite3 binary from nix directly\n/nix/store/pfaam25wgs7ls2bfmq8mdqdmnhbk7j2f-sqlite-3.51.2-bin/bin/sqlite3 --version\n```\n\n### \"Restore failed\" - Network Issues\n\nTest ARMOR endpoint connectivity:\n\n```bash\n# Test basic connectivity\ncurl -I http://100.80.255.8:9000\n\n# Expected response: HTTP/1.1 200 OK or similar\n# If connection refused/timeout: ARMOR may be down or unreachable\n```\n\nCheck if ARMOR is running:\n\n```bash\n# If you have kubectl access to ord-devimprint\nkubectl get pods -n devimprint -l app=armor\n\n# Check ARMOR logs\nkubectl logs -n devimprint -l app=armor --tail=50\n```\n\n### \"Restore failed\" - Authentication Issues\n\nVerify credentials are correct:\n\n```bash\n# Test credentials with AWS CLI (if available)\naws s3 ls \\\n  --endpoint-url http://100.80.255.8:9000 \\\n  --access-key-id $LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID \\\n  --secret-key $LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\n\n# Should list buckets including \"devimprint\"\n```\n\n### \"Restore failed\" - Path Issues\n\nVerify the backup path exists:\n\n```bash\n# List objects in the bucket\naws s3 ls s3://devimprint/state/litestream/ \\\n  --endpoint-url http://100.80.255.8:9000 \\\n  --access-key-id $LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID \\\n  --secret-key $LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\n\n# Should see queue.db and related litestream metadata files\n```\n\n### Database Corruption\n\nIf the restored database fails integrity checks:\n\n```bash\n# Run full integrity check\nsqlite3 ./restored/queue.db \"PRAGMA integrity_check;\"\n\n# If errors reported, try restoring an older backup\n# List available generations in litestream metadata\n```\n\n### ARMOR Endpoint Returns 403/401\n\n- **403 Forbidden**: Check bucket permissions, verify `armor-writer` credentials include read access\n- **401 Unauthorized**: Invalid credentials, verify `LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID` and `LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY`\n- **Connection timeout**: ARMOR endpoint unreachable, check network connectivity and service status\n\n## Architecture\n\n### Production Setup\n\n```\nqueue-api Pod (devimprint namespace)\n├── queue-api container\n│   └── /data/queue.db (SQLite database)\n└── litestream sidecar\n    ├── Reads: /data/queue.db\n    ├── Replicates to: ARMOR S3 endpoint\n    └── ARMOR endpoint: http://100.80.255.8:9000\n         └── Bucket: devimprint\n            └── Path: state/litestream/queue.db\n               └── ARMOR encrypts → Backblaze B2 (ciphertext)\n\nPVC: queue-api-data-sata-2\nSecret: armor-writer (contains ARMOR auth credentials)\n```\n\n### ARMOR in the Data Flow\n\n```\n┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌──────────────┐     ┌─────────────┐\n│ queue-api    │────▶│ litestream   │────▶│ ARMOR        │────▶│ Backblaze B2│\n│              │     │ (sidecar)    │     │ (encrypts)   │     │ (storage)   │\n└──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └──────────────┘     └─────────────┘\n                             │                     │\n                             └────restore─────────┘\n                                    │\n                        ┌───────────▼────────────┐\n                        │ fresh-restore env      │\n                        │ ./restore.sh           │\n                        │ ↓                      │\n                        │ ./restored/queue.db    │\n                        └────────────────────────┘\n```\n\n### Restore Environment\n\n```\n/home/coding/scratch/fresh-restore/\n├── restore.sh              # Generic litestream restore script\n├── README.md               # This file\n├── bf-2ke2y-status.md      # Previous implementation status\n└── restored/               # Created during restore\n    └── queue.db            # Restored database\n```\n\n## Testing ARMOR Connectivity\n\nBefore attempting a restore, verify you can connect to the ARMOR endpoint:\n\n### 1. Basic Connectivity Test\n\n```bash\n# Test if ARMOR endpoint is reachable\ncurl -I http://100.80.255.8:9000\n\n# Expected: HTTP/1.1 200 OK or similar\n# Connection refused/timeout: ARMOR is down or unreachable\n```\n\n### 2. S3 List Test (requires credentials)\n\n```bash\n# List buckets (verify credentials work)\naws s3 ls \\\n  --endpoint-url http://100.80.255.8:9000 \\\n  --access-key-id $LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID \\\n  --secret-key $LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\n\n# Should see \"devimprint\" bucket\n```\n\n### 3. List Backup Files\n\n```bash\n# List litestream backup files\naws s3 ls s3://devimprint/state/litestream/ \\\n  --endpoint-url http://100.80.255.8:9000 \\\n  --access-key-id $LITESTREAM_ACCESS_KEY_ID \\\n  --secret-key $LITESTREAM_SECRET_ACCESS_KEY\n\n# Should see queue.db and litestream metadata files\n```\n\n### 4. Test litestream directly\n\n```bash\n# Test litestream can connect\nlitestream restore s3://devimprint/state/litestream/queue.db -o /tmp/test.db -v\n\n# Should download and restore (remove -v for less verbosity)\n```\n\n## Safety\n\nThis restore environment is **completely isolated** from production:\n\n- ✅ Does not affect the running queue-api deployment\n- ✅ Does not modify the PVC (`queue-api-data-sata-2`)\n- ✅ Does not affect S3 backups\n- ✅ Uses separate scratch directory\n- ✅ Read-only operations against S3 (except for restore.sh which only downloads)\n\n## What Makes This \"Fresh\"?\n\nThis is called a \"fresh\" restore because:\n1. **New scratch location**: Uses `/home/coding/scratch/fresh-restore/` instead of the existing `/home/coding/scratch/restore-test/`\n2. **Clean restore**: Downloads the latest backup from S3 without any local caching\n3. **Up-to-date**: Gets the most recent backup including any recent changes\n\n## Related Documentation\n\n### ARMOR Documentation\n- **ARMOR GitHub**: https://github.com/jedarden/ARMOR - Main ARMOR repository\n- **ARMOR README**: `/home/coding/ARMOR/README.md` - Full ARMOR documentation including:\n  - S3 API compatibility\n  - Encryption design\n  - Disaster recovery\n  - Admin API endpoints\n  - Dashboard usage\n- **Disaster Recovery**: `/home/coding/ARMOR/docs/disaster-recovery.md` - MEK backup and restore procedures\n\n### Litestream Documentation\n- **Litestream docs**: https://litestream.io - Has additional scripts and Makefile for queue-api restores\n\n### ARMOR Admin Endpoints\nIf you need to debug ARMOR directly (requires access to the cluster):\n\n| Endpoint | Method | Purpose |\n|----------|--------|---------|\n| `/healthz` | GET | Liveness check |\n| `/readyz` | GET | Readiness check (B2 connectivity) |\n| `/metrics` | GET | Prometheus metrics |\n| `/admin/key/verify` | GET | Verify MEK can decrypt canary |\n| `/dashboard` | GET | Web dashboard for browsing |\n| `/armor/canary` | GET | Canary integrity status |\n\nAccess via kubectl:\n```bash\n# Port-forward to access admin endpoints locally\nkubectl port-forward -n devimprint svc/armor 9001:9001\n\n# Then access:\n# -
